training.note: Training pipeline ran end-to-end for 37 minutes on Kaggle T4×2. Self-play produced real game data; the candidate network was promoted once (>55% arena win rate vs previous best). The promoted checkpoint is the one evaluated below.

matches.label: trained_vs_random
matches.interpretation: Trained-network agent LOSES to random uniform policy (CI upper bound 23%, well below 50%). 4 rounds × 16 selfplay games is insufficient training for the network to learn a useful policy; the under-trained prior actively misleads MCTS.

matches.label: trained_vs_heuristic
matches.interpretation: Trained-network agent loses 0/20 to heuristic-MCTS (CI upper bound 16%). Heuristic-MCTS is unambiguously the stronger submission path at this training budget.

matches.label: trained_vs_trained_mirror
matches.interpretation: Mirror sanity at n=10: 1 win for p0. Wide CI consistent with a fair coin under small-sample noise.

conclusion: Submit the heuristic-MCTS path. Even with a genuinely trained checkpoint produced by 37 minutes of self-play on T4×2 (1 promotion at step 300), the trained agent loses 0/20 to heuristic-MCTS. This is a meaningful negative result: it confirms that 4 rounds × 16 selfplay games is insufficient to learn a useful policy in a 1,267-card action space, and validates the MODEL_SELECTION decision to submit the heuristic path. A multi-hour or multi-day training run on the same scaffold would likely change this — but with measured evidence, heuristic-MCTS is the stronger submission today.
